When we have seen similar health-related shocks in the past, typically they have been followed by very sharp rebounds. Clearly this is much worse than SARS, but for Hong Kong the impact of SARS was severe at the time (it was estimated at the time that 25% of the population would be infected, with a mortality rate of 11% - although in fact less than 0.01% of the population was infected). However, if we look at Cathay Pacific or Hong Kong real estate, we see that initial underperformance of 33% and 27% was offset by subsequent rebounds within six and four months, respectively. (In fact, from the low, Hong Kong Real Estate in absolute terms fell 28% and then rose 80% over the following six months).
